topic_description: Oxygen loop comes online; a first crisis creeps closer.

content_blocks.content: Yesterday we locked the power baseline at 67%, and the 24-hour sample is running. Today the crew bring the oxygen loop online for real—electrolysis, CO₂ scrubbing, cabin partial pressures. It sounds like "push a button," but every breath is tied to power. Chen Wei, start with the numbers: three people, one lunar day—how do we budget O₂ and CO₂?

This episode we call "Between Breaths." Breathing—something we never think about on Earth—becomes a string of numbers and valves in the cabin. The first crisis isn't an explosion; it's a curve drifting up: CO₂ concentration, or oxygen partial pressure dipping. Life and fragility sit in the space between this breath and the next.

Adult resting O₂ consumption is about 3.5 mL/(min·kg) body mass. For three people at 70 kg each, that's roughly 0.84 kg O₂ per day. CO₂ output follows the respiratory quotient RQ ≈ 0.85, so about 0.72 kg CO₂ per day. Cabin volume we take as 120 m³. To keep ppO₂ at 20–22 kPa and ppCO₂ below 0.5 kPa we need continuous O₂ makeup and continuous CO₂ removal.
\dot{m}_{\text{O}_2} \approx \frac{3 \times 70\,\text{kg} \times 3.5\,\text{mL/(min·kg)} \times 60\times24}{10^6} \times \rho_{\text{O}_2} \approx 0.84\,\text{kg/day}

Electrolysis: 2H₂O → O₂ + 2H₂. Per kg of O₂ we need to electrolyze about 1.125 kg water; energy is about 4–5 kWh/kg O₂ (real systems with pumps and losses run higher—we budget 5.5 kWh/kg). So O₂ alone is about 4.6 kWh per day for three, over 10% of daily generation at 67% power.
The water in the tank feeds both electrolysis and drinking. O₂ production and drinking have to share the same water budget. We can't run the electrolyzer and starve drinking water.
In the daily brief we can split two lines: water for O₂, water for drinking and other. So they see both sides of the consumption.
So "breathing" isn't free. Every breath they take is paid in power. Yesterday they were reeling from 67%—today they have to run their lungs on that same 67%.

CO₂ removal is an adsorption loop—lithium hydroxide or molecular sieve. When it's saturated it has to be regenerated; regeneration needs heat; heat needs power. So CO₂ and O₂ are two sides of the same ledger: making O₂ costs power, scrubbing CO₂ costs power. Both have to fit inside the 67% budget.

Yesterday they learned where the power comes from and whether it's enough; today they learn where the gas comes from and whether it's enough. Once both ledgers are clear, they move from "surviving" toward "living."

Life-support power is already top priority in the system, but we need to spell it out: after they bring the loop up today, the panel will show two curves—cabin ppO₂ and ppCO₂. Normally both stay in the green. If either enters the yellow zone, they follow the procedure—no waiting.
Green, yellow, red thresholds go in the flight rules like L1/L2. On the surface we don't change them on the fly—we execute. So crew and ground agree on what "caution" and "emergency" mean.
p_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{n_{\text{CO}_2} R T}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{if } V=120\,\text{m}^3,\; T=295\,\text{K},\; \dot{n}_{\text{CO}_2}\text{ constant then } p_{\text{CO}_2} \text{ rises}

If removal can't keep up, ppCO₂ drifts up. Short-term human tolerance is around 1 kPa; above that, headache, drowsiness, impaired judgment. So we set yellow at 0.4 kPa and red at 0.6 kPa. At yellow they increase scrubbing or switch to backup canisters—don't wait for red.

content_blocks.description: O₂ and CO₂ loop schematic: electrolyzer, water tank, O₂ into cabin; CO₂ adsorber, regeneration heating, gas loop; ppO₂/ppCO₂ monitoring and green/yellow/red zones.

content_blocks.content: Pipe and valve labels have to be clear: which line is O₂, which is H₂, which is CO₂ out. All three crew need to know—not just one person who can operate it.
The first time they watch those two curves they'll think: every breath we take is on there. That feeling—not fear, but a sharp awareness that we're living on machinery.
Keep at least 24 hours of curve history. If something goes wrong we can see whether it was a slow drift or a sudden jump. Helps debrief and update procedures.

Electrolyzer startup sequence goes in the checklist: confirm water level and line seals, then power up; run at low load for a few minutes, then ramp to design flow. Going full blast at once can trip protection and waste time.

graph LR
    subgraph Input
        W[Water tank]
    end
    subgraph O2 gen
        E[Electrolyzer]
    end
    subgraph Cabin
        C[Cabin 120m³]
    end
    subgraph Scrub
        S[CO₂ adsorb/regenerate]
    end
    W --> E
    E -->|O₂| C
    C -->|CO₂| S
    S --> C
    style E fill:#eab30833
    style S fill:#64748b33

When the electrolyzer comes on, the cabin gets a low hum. They have to get used to it—that's the sound of oxygen being made. Like yesterday's power curve, it becomes background, but it must never really be background.

Before first startup they can recite one line: we are bringing the oxygen loop online, executing per checklist, breathing first. Same idea as yesterday's "we're collecting data"—a psychological anchor.

At 67% power, daily generation is on the order of 30 kWh. Life support—O₂ plus CO₂ plus fans and thermal—roughly 8–10 kWh. The rest is comms, science, lighting, thermal. So the L1/L2 shed order from yesterday holds: when power is tight, breathing first, then everything else.
In the system we can make "life support" a non-sheddable load group. At L2 we shed science and entertainment; at L1 we keep only life support and comms. That way no one can accidentally shut off O₂.
If O₂ and CO₂ removal stop, how long can the cabin hold? Depends on leak and volume. Roughly, with a good seal, a few to a dozen hours. So when the alarm sounds, reaction time is in hours, not days.
"Breathing first"—they'll hear it a lot today. Not to scare them, but so they know: if the alarm goes off, they know what to protect first.

The procedure can add a line: when there's a breathing alert, drop whatever else you're doing. Not that other things don't matter—but if breathing goes wrong, minutes can be irreversible. Priority has to be instinct.

We have two CO₂ canisters so we can rotate: one on absorb, one regenerating. Track the regeneration cycle so we don't have both at end of life. Checklist: every 8 hours check canister status; when one is near saturation, switch and start regeneration.

We can add a "breathing status" card on the panel: current ppO₂, ppCO₂, electrolyzer power, today's O₂ produced, today's CO₂ removed. One glance to see if it's normal. Plus an alert: if either partial pressure leaves the green zone, push to main screen and voice.
Alert levels: yellow is "caution," red is "emergency." At caution they have 15 minutes to act or escalate; at emergency they follow procedure immediately and notify the ground.
When a canister is regenerating there's a short heating pulse—cabin thermal load may tick up slightly. Thermal control should know so they don't misread it as a fault.

Water is the feedstock for O₂. Water tank level goes in the daily brief—how much used, how much left, how many days at current use. If water is tight, we have to ration O₂ production—reduce activity or accept a slightly lower cabin ppO₂ within safe bounds.
Water level sensors have to be reliable. If we misread "plenty left" when we're near empty, we're in trouble. Checklist: daily visual or secondary confirmation of level, cross-check with sensor.
"How many days at current use"—that number gives them a boundary. Knowing where the line is avoids both unconscious waste and pointless anxiety.

The first kind of crisis—the one that "creeps closer"—is usually a curve drifting. No one shouts, no explosion, just a number slowly going wrong. So they need to get used to watching the curves, not waiting for the alarm. The alarm is the last line of defense.
We can add a "trend alert": if the curve moves the wrong way for 30 minutes straight, warn before it hits yellow. That gives a longer response window.

If the electrolyzer fails we have backup O₂ bottles—high-pressure gaseous, enough for the three for several days. That's emergency only, not routine. Routine is electrolysis. So electrolyzer maintenance and inspection go in the daily must-do, same weight as the power brief.

After they bring the loop up today, log ppO₂, ppCO₂, and electrolyzer power every 15 minutes for the first two hours. Send the data to the ground; we both look at whether the curves are stable. Once stable, switch to once per hour. That way we don't have a blind spot, but we're not staring nonstop.
Those first two hours of dense logging also signal "we're taking this seriously." Not just pushing a button—every step is on the record.
P_{\text{electrolysis}} \approx 5.5\,\text{kWh/kg}_{\text{O}_2} \times \dot{m}_{\text{O}_2} \approx 5.5 \times \frac{0.84}{24}\,\text{kW} \approx 0.19\,\text{kW} \quad \text{(steady-state O2 power)}

In practice we have pumps, control, cooling—total power for O₂ is around 0.3 kW. CO₂ scrubbing fans and regeneration add a bit more; the whole breathing slice of life support is about 0.5–0.6 kW continuous. That's acceptable at 67% power.
When they finish startup they might have a moment of silence: listening to the fans, watching the numbers go green. That moment is "between breaths"—we've hooked up this breath.
Green isn't "perfect," it's "safe." ppO₂ 20–22 kPa, ppCO₂ below 0.4 kPa is green. If they know the bounds they won't stress over the second decimal.
The ground will get the same breathing data today. Both sides see the same curves—the crew won't feel like they're carrying it alone. Same as yesterday's power data—shared reality.

If there's a pressure difference between the airlock and the cabin, equalize before opening the inner hatch. Otherwise airflow can pull in dust. So the pre-EVA checklist gets a line: confirm airlock and cabin pressure equal, then open inner hatch. Breathing and sealing go together.

Three lines for the crew: one, today we bring the oxygen loop online—when ppO₂ and ppCO₂ are in the green, breathing is stable; two, both O₂ and CO₂ removal use power, so within the 67% budget we protect breathing first; three, if either curve enters yellow, follow the procedure, act first then report.
Those three lines can go at the top of today's brief, like yesterday's power three. When they open it they see the breathing baseline first.
At handover recite breathing status: current ppO₂ and ppCO₂ in green, electrolyzer and scrubbing nominal. Next shift takes that in—same as power and sealing, make it habit.
Right. And tomorrow we can tune from today's data: if actual O₂ power is lower than we thought, we have margin; if it's near the limit we remind them to save power and avoid nonessential load.
The ground will see the same breathing data today. When the crew report "loop is up, numbers normal," ground answers "copy, we see the curves too"—confirm we're on the same page.
When they lie down tonight they may run through: power 67%, gas hooked up, both curves in green. Three anchors steady—then they can close their eyes.
When they lie down tonight, breathing will feel different—because they know where that breath comes from and how. Life and fragility isn't a slogan; it's a daily ledger.
Backup O₂ bottle pressure and expiry go in the daily brief too. If electrolysis fails, how long we can hold and when the ground could resupply—they need to know.
At handover add a breathing status line: current ppO₂ and ppCO₂ in green, electrolyzer and scrubbing nominal. The next shift takes that in, same as the power recitation.
If water only lasts N days, the brief should say "at current O₂ rate, water lasts N days." They need that line to decide whether to save water and power.

content_blocks.description: Cabin atmosphere panel concept: ppO₂ and ppCO₂ real-time curves, green/yellow/red zones, electrolyzer power and today's O₂/CO₂ numbers.

content_blocks.content: If we ever write this episode, the title is already there: "Between Breaths." Not the gap between one breath and the next—but that every breath is tied to a thread, and the thread runs to power, water, and the three of them: shared fragility, shared resolve.
Tomorrow Ice and Fire will bring thermal and maybe injury. Today we stabilize both breathing and power; tomorrow we have headroom for the next variable. One gate at a time—don't stack them.

The first crisis "creeping closer"—we can play it as: this afternoon ppCO₂ drifts up, removal slightly below production, touching 0.35 kPa. They follow procedure, switch canisters, increase fan; half an hour later the curve comes down. No casualties, but they get the reality: we really are breathing on machinery.
Keep the full data for that "mini crisis": rise, actions taken, fall. Useful for training and debrief, and for the mission report.

The procedure has to spell out: who watches the curves, who does the switch, who talks to the ground. All three can't be at the valves, and someone has to be in charge. Same as yesterday's load shed—clear roles.

The dense two-hour data from the ground can build a simple breathing model: actual O₂ consumption, scrubber efficiency, effective cabin mixing. Then we can predict "how long we can hold" with more confidence.
They should have drilled canister switch in the simulator: ppCO₂ into yellow, switch canister, start regeneration. So on the surface they don't fumble.
If that drift happens, don't blame anyone after. Frame it as "the system was running at the edge and we followed procedure." "We handled it" not "we almost had a problem"—that mindset matters.
Right. And 0.35 kPa is still well below red—a harmless drill. At 0.5 it would be too late to learn.
That ppCO₂ drift—when they debrief they'll say: we followed procedure and the curve came back. That "we did it" is worth more than any reassurance.
Log the response in the mission log: when it hit yellow, who decided, who operated, when it came down. So we have a record for reports and training.
\text{Removal rate } \dot{V}_{\text{CO}_2} \geq \dot{n}_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot \frac{RT}{p} \quad \text{for steady state; else } p_{\text{CO}_2} \uparrow
So canister throughput and regeneration frequency have to match the three's CO₂ output. We sized it in design, but actual metabolic rate on the surface may vary slightly—today's data will validate.
"Match" sounds technical—for them it's: switch canisters on schedule, run regeneration on schedule, numbers stay stable. Turn the complex into checklist ticks and the mental load drops.
Electrolyzer H₂ has to be vented outside or burned in a reactor—it can't accumulate in the cabin. H₂ vent pressure and leak checks go on the checklist.
If H₂ leaks into the cabin and mixes with O₂ to a certain ratio it can explode. So H₂ vent leak checks and "H₂ concentration monitoring" go together—can't rely on visual only.
If the reactor burns H₂ to water, that water can go back into the O₂ loop. The tighter the loop, the slower we use water—better for the long run.
In the daily brief put "breathing status" second—power first. So every day they see power, then gas. Both ledgers clear, minds at ease.
On the breathing resource card add a line: at current use, water lasts X days, backup O₂ lasts Y hours. Update the numbers daily so they have expectation.
Tomorrow is "Ice and Fire"—temperature extremes, equipment failure, maybe someone hurt. Today we hook up breathing; tomorrow we're ready for the next test. One gate at a time.
Breathing and thermal are linked—higher cabin temp, slightly higher metabolism and CO₂. Stable temp, stable breathing load. So tomorrow's episode ties into today's ledger too.
If the H₂-O₂ reactor burns H₂, the product is water—we can return it to the water tank. So we don't waste H₂ and we gain a bit of water for electrolysis. Close the loop as much as we can.

content_blocks.description: ppCO₂ drift and response: time on x-axis, ppCO₂ (kPa) on y-axis; curve rises to 0.35, then falls after canister switch; yellow threshold and response point labeled.

content_blocks.content: Water level, O₂ bottle pressure, canister status—those three can be a "breathing resources" card next to the power card. One glance to know if they can breathe easy today.
"Breathe easy"—on Earth nobody thinks that. In the cabin it's something to confirm every day. Acknowledge that difference and they'll take every checkbox seriously.
On the breathing resource card add: next planned canister switch. So they know "how long until we have to act" and don't forget.

If the crew ask the ground today: we've brought the loop up, numbers look good, what next? Ground can answer: keep monitoring, first two hours report at 15-minute intervals, then relax. No new tasks—just hold this breath steady.
Don't schedule EVA or heavy work on startup day. The breathing system is settling into its load and the crew into the new noise and rhythm. One steady day before we push.
"Between breaths" is also between us and those three. They breathe, we run the numbers. They watch the curves, we watch the same curves. Same ledger—then we get through together.

The ground shouldn't pile on new suggestions today. Today we only do three things: bring the loop up, watch the curves, respond per procedure. Other ideas get written down for tomorrow. Same as yesterday's power—stabilize one day, then expand.

Electrolyzer efficiency varies a bit with water temp and quality. Long term we log actual kWh per kg O₂ and compare to the 5.5 kWh/kg baseline. If it drifts, we troubleshoot: electrodes, membrane, pumps.

Tonight they may think: every breath we take is paid in power and water. That feeling—fragility, but also clarity. Life and fragility isn't negative; it's fact. Accept the fact and the procedure becomes real.
If someone is hurt tomorrow in Ice and Fire, medical O₂ comes from the same system. So stabilizing breathing today means we're ready for both cabin atmosphere and emergency O₂ tomorrow.
That's it for today. Oxygen loop startup, breathing status card, ppCO₂ procedure and resource brief—we've set what we can. The rest is up to them and the ground.
When they fall asleep tonight to the sound of the fans, may they think "we hooked up this breath"—not "we almost didn't." Camp Zero, Day 3. See you tomorrow.

Tomorrow Ice and Fire will cover thermal and possible injury. Thermal and breathing both use power, so today's 67% and breathing baseline are the premise for "how we keep people safe in extreme temps."
If the crew ask today "what do we do next," ground answers: keep monitoring, first two hours at 15-minute intervals, then relax. No new tasks. Same as yesterday—stabilize this page first.
Tomorrow we talk Ice and Fire: extreme temps, equipment failure, possible injury. Stabilize breathing today so we have capital for the next wave.

content_blocks.description: "Between Breaths" concept: three crew in cabin, O₂ loop and ppO₂/ppCO₂ curves in background; warm light and green-zone numbers, calm but tense.
Electrolyzer and CO₂ canisters: electrolyzer unit, water/O₂ ports; dual canisters with regeneration heating; maintenance check points labeled.
